turns.content: I've read about Pin and Future but I still don't get how to actually run a future without an async runtime. I thought I could just call the poll method on a Boxed dyn Future and it would work like normal code.

That's a common misunderstanding; you cannot simply invoke Poll manually because the Future trait requires a context and waker to track external events. When you Pin<Box<dyn Future>> in your executor, that boxed value holds the future state but lacks the runtime machinery needed for proper polling. You need to build a custom executor that manages its own context and waker so it can call Poll correctly and handle wake scheduling when other tasks are ready.
So I'm supposed to write my own event loop? Like, manually track which futures have been polled enough times?

Exactly, you are building a simple executor that maintains state for every active Future. Your loop must repeatedly Poll each pinned future until it returns Ready or the task is cancelled, then update your internal list of pending tasks to reflect their current state.
Wait, I thought Pin just ensures ownership so I can move futures around freely? Isn't that enough?

Pin only guarantees that a value cannot be moved out from under you while it is pinned, but it does not provide the polling mechanism itself. You still need to implement the logic that calls Poll on your Pin<Box<dyn Future>> instances and manages their lifecycle within your executor.
Okay so my executor needs to store context and waker for each future I'm running. Is there a standard way to do this or should I build it from scratch?

There is no standard library function that does this automatically because the runtime itself is what provides those mechanisms. You must build a simple executor structure that holds your collection of futures and their associated context and waker data, then implement the main loop to drive Poll on each one.
So I'm basically implementing the event loop manually since Rust doesn't give me a built-in way to do this?

You are implementing the core logic of an async runtime from scratch. You need to manage wake scheduling so that when your executor detects new events, it can update the waker on any pending futures and force them to be polled again in the next iteration.
That sounds like a lot of work for just running code? Do you have an analogy I can use to understand why we need all this manual setup?

Think of it like a kitchen where you are the chef rather than using pre-prepared appliances. A standard async runtime is like having automatic blenders that run on timers, but building your own executor means you are manually controlling when each future gets processed and ensuring they wake up correctly when needed.

I built a tool to check if my server is down. I wrote code where I hold Pin<Box<dyn Future>> in a box, then use context and waker to wake it up when the timer fires. But this feels messy and I'm worried about memory safety.

It's understandable that you feel this is messy because you're trying to manage Pin<Box<dyn Future>> manually without a proper runtime. The core issue here is that you are holding onto the waker and context yourself instead of letting the executor own them, which breaks the abstraction layer between your code and the async runtime.
Yes, I'm trying to build a simple executor myself. I think if I wake scheduling manually by calling poll on the future, it should work fine without needing an external library like tokio.

Building a simple executor that calls poll directly is technically possible but introduces race conditions because you are bypassing the standard async control flow. When you manually wake scheduling, you lose the guarantee that the future will not be polled twice simultaneously, which leads to logic errors.
I understand about the double poll risk, but I need this to run on my own machine without dependencies. Is there a way to pin the Box<dyn Future> and manage the waker myself safely?

You can Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and manage the context manually, but you must ensure that all state mutations are protected by a lock or done via Send/Sync traits. The real problem is not just pinning but how you handle the waker; if your executor panics while holding the future's internal state, it breaks the entire program.
That sounds scary. I feel like I'm going to crash my system if I don't get this right. Can you explain why the standard library doesn't just give us a manual executor?

The standard library provides abstractions because they handle the complexity of thread safety and state management for you. When you implement your own wake scheduling, you are essentially reimplementing parts of the runtime that were designed to be robust against exactly these kinds of manual errors.
I don't want to give up though. What if I just use a loop and call Poll on the future until it returns Ready? Is there any specific trait or function I need to watch out for?

You can implement a polling loop that calls Poll until Ready, but you must ensure your code respects the bounds of the dyn Future trait and handles panics gracefully. The key is understanding how context and waker interact with the executor; if you don't pass them correctly through the impl Async trait, you won't get expected behavior.

Okay, so I should probably stop trying to build my own executor and just use something like tokio or std::thread instead. But why does Rust force me to deal with Pin<Box<dyn Future>> if the standard library handles it?

Rust forces you to deal with Pin<Box<dyn Future>> because it ensures that async code can be safely shared across threads and reused in different contexts. The standard library abstracts this away so you don't have to think about the details of waker management, but understanding them helps when you need to debug performance issues or write custom runtimes.
I'm getting this error: 'the trait bound for dyn Future is not satisfied' when trying to pin a generic future. What went wrong?

The issue is that you cannot directly `Pin` a raw `dyn Future` without boxing it first or using the Pin trait on a concrete type. You need to wrap your dynamic future in a Box so it becomes `Box<dyn Future>`, then pass that to `Pin::new()`. The compiler needs a concrete size and layout, which only exists after boxing.
Okay, I boxed it with Pin::new(Box::new(my_future)). But now my executor isn't waking up correctly. How do I handle the waker?

When you manually poll a future in an executor, you must explicitly create a Waker object to signal when the future should run again. In your simple executor loop, after calling `poll()`, if it returns Poll::Pending, you need to store that waker and schedule the next wake event. You can use a channel or a custom scheduler struct that holds the waker alongside the pinned future.
I tried using Arc<Pin<Box<dyn Future>>> but got lifetime issues. What's the correct way to store this in my executor state?

Avoid `Arc` for raw pointers since it doesn't implement `Unpin`. Instead, use a struct that holds an `Option<Pin<Box<dyn Future>>>` and a shared waker reference. Your executor state should look like a simple tuple or struct containing the pinned future and a mutable Waker guard so you can update it when scheduling wakeups.
I see. So I need to manually manage the waker in my custom executor loop. Can you show me how to structure that polling logic?

In your main thread, create a struct with a field for `Pin<Box<dyn Future>>` and another for a `Waker`. In your poll loop, call `future.as_ref().poll()` to check progress. If it returns Pending, use the waker's register method to add yourself to the scheduling queue. When the runtime wakes you again, resume polling from where you left off.
This feels like I'm reinventing the thread pool. Is there a simpler way without building this manually?

For general use, yes—the `tokio::spawn` macro handles pinning and wakers automatically via its internal runtime. But if you're learning low-level async or building a custom runtime like `async-std`'s executor, then manually managing the Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and Waker gives you full control over scheduling decisions.
I want to understand why we need Pin::new here. Does it just prevent data races?

`Pin` ensures that a reference to data can't move in ways the compiler doesn't expect, which prevents undefined behavior during async operations. Without `Pin`, you could accidentally reassign the future pointer while it's being polled, breaking the contract of the Future trait. So yes, it guards against races and invalid mutations.
Got it. Now I'm trying to implement a simple executor that runs multiple futures concurrently. How do I track which ones are running?

Use a list or set of active tasks, each holding its own `Pin<Box<dyn Future>>` and waker. In your event loop, pick one task from the ready state, call poll on it, and if it's pending again, update its waker to signal when it should be picked next. This mimics how real runtimes manage concurrent work.
What happens if a future panics? Does my custom executor catch that?

If a future panics, it will propagate up unless you wrap the poll call in a `catch_unwind` or similar handler. In your simple executor, you should catch panic from each task and optionally rethrow it to the main thread or log it before continuing with other tasks.
I'm struggling with the waker registration part. What exactly do I call when a future is pending?

You call `waker.register()` on your custom waker object to tell it that this particular future needs to wake up later. This registers an entry in your internal scheduling queue so the executor knows when to resume polling this specific future.

I'm trying to build a tiny executor that runs async code without using tokio, but I keep hitting a wall with the Pin trait. Specifically, I need to store a Future inside my executor state so it can be polled later.

You're dealing with the core problem of ownership and pinning in async code. If you just hold a raw pointer or an Rc<RefCell<dyn Future>>, your compiler will panic because the data might move while you try to poll it, violating the Pin requirement.
That sounds like I should use a Pin<Box<dyn Future>> but isn't that just for the static case? Can I really pin a generic Future trait object inside my runtime?

You can absolutely pin any dynamic future, and Pin<Box<dyn Future>> is the standard way to do it. The key insight is that Box implements DerefMut which lets you get a reference without moving the underlying value, satisfying the requirement for Poll to take a mutable reference.
Wait, so I just wrap my future in Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and store it? But what if the user code mutates state that the future needs access to?

Exactly, and that's where the context comes in. Your executor isn't just holding a Future; it needs to hold the associated data structures like counters or buffers so the future can poll them safely without needing to move itself.
So my struct looks something like Executor { context: ..., futures: Vec<Pin<Box<dyn Future>>>, }? And I just call poll on each one?

That's the right shape, but you need to be careful about how you manage the waker. When a future is ready or blocked, it needs to tell your executor to wake up later if nothing else is running, otherwise you'll have an infinite loop of polling.
I thought the waker was just for notifying the runtime. How do I actually schedule a wake event in my manual executor?

In your manual executor, you maintain a list of pending futures with their associated wakers. When a future returns Poll::Ready, you check if it set a waker; if so, you add that waker to your scheduling queue and mark the future as done.
But what happens when I have multiple futures? Do I poll them all in sequence or do I interleave based on readiness?

You should implement a round-robin or priority-based scheduler. For simplicity, just keep polling the next ready future until one blocks and sets a waker, then you can switch to that waker's callback later.
One last thing I'm confused about: if I use Pin<Box<dyn Future>> everywhere, does that mean I lose the ability to inspect the concrete type of the future?

You do lose direct access to the concrete implementation details unless you implement a trait like Debug or specific bounds. But for most executors, treating them uniformly as dyn Future is fine since they only need the Poll method and any context data.
I've read about Pin and dyn Future but I still don't get how to actually poll them manually in an executor. It feels like I'm missing something fundamental about the lifecycle.

The confusion usually comes from thinking a Future is just a static block of code, but it's really a trait object that needs to be polled repeatedly until it returns Ok or Err. When you Pin<Box<dyn Future>> in your executor, you're taking ownership so the future can't move, which prevents double-frees and ensures the Waker only fires once per task.
So if I have a Pin<Box<dyn Future>>, how do I actually call Poll on it without using async/await?

You need to manually invoke the Poll method on the boxed future, passing in your current context and any pending wake sources. The executor loop should keep calling poll until the result is ready or an error occurs, then handle the waker registration when you're done with that specific task.
Can you show me a minimal example where I construct the Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and then manually call Poll in a loop?

Sure. You'd define your simple future struct that implements Future, then wrap it in Box<dyn Future> using Pin::new. Inside your executor loop, you call the poll method on the boxed value, passing in the current execution context and any pending wake sources to track when tasks should resume.
I see the code now, but why do I need to pass the waker into the Poll implementation instead of just returning it?

Because the executor needs to know which task is waiting so it can schedule wakeups. When you poll a Future that returns Ok(()) and registers a Waker, the executor stores that reference and only restarts polling when the associated event fires or another future completes.
Ah okay, but what exactly happens inside Poll if I return Ok(()) versus Err(E)? How does the executor distinguish between completion and cancellation?

If you return Ok(T), the executor consumes that value and moves on to the next task. If you return Err(E), it means the future was aborted or completed early, so your executor should handle cleanup and potentially notify any waiting tasks. The key is that Poll never returns a reference; it always returns ownership of whatever data the Future produces.
I think I understand now, but can we talk about how this fits into larger systems? Like what happens when I have multiple futures running at once?

When you have multiple Pin<Box<dyn Future>> instances in your executor, you maintain a list of pending tasks. Each time one completes via Ok(T), you remove it from the list and update its state. The waker mechanism ensures that even if many futures are blocked, they only wake up when their specific event occurs.
That makes sense, but what about error handling? If one of my futures panics or returns an Err during Poll, how does the executor propagate that?

If a future panics in its Poll implementation, your executor needs to catch it and handle the panic gracefully. For Err(E) returns from Poll, you should check if the error is recoverable and potentially retry or notify other waiting tasks before moving on to the next iteration of the loop.
Okay so I'm building a simple executor with context and waker right? How do I ensure that my Pin<Box<dyn Future>> stays pinned throughout its lifetime?

You use std::pin::Pin::new() on your future before boxing it. This guarantees the pointer remains valid even after the future is moved, which is critical because Poll doesn't allow the data to move between calls unless you're using Pin.
Got it. But wait, what if I'm using tokio or some other runtime? Does my manual polling approach conflict with their built-in async support?

Manual polling complements runtimes like tokio by giving you finer control over the execution flow. You can still use std::future::Future and Pin<Box<dyn Future>> in your code, but instead of relying on runtime scheduling, you implement your own lightweight executor that polls futures directly using Poll.
This is getting clearer now. One last thing - how do I integrate this into a real application where users might want to spawn tasks dynamically?

You can wrap the manual polling logic in a function that accepts any number of Pin<Box<dyn Future>> instances and their associated wakers. Each time a user wants to start a new task, you simply add it to your executor's pending list and let the loop handle scheduling.
Perfect! So my next step is to write a small test harness that simulates multiple futures waking up at different times using this manual approach?

Exactly. Create a simple test that defines several Pin<Box<dyn Future>> instances, each with its own waker, then run your executor loop and verify they wake up in the correct order without relying on external timers.

I've been reading about Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and how it relates to the executor, but I still don't get why we need this specific pinning strategy when building a simple executor. Can you walk me through it?

You've read the basics which is great, but there's often confusion about whether Pin<Box<dyn Future>> actually changes how futures are polled versus just being a type constraint. The key difference is that without this pinning, the future could escape its context and break the executor's ability to manage the waker correctly.
So it's not about performance but about safety? I thought Pin was just for borrowing rules?

It is primarily for borrow safety, but in this specific context of polling futures manually, that safety requirement forces us to use a boxed dyn Future because the compiler can't guarantee the future stays within our executor's scope otherwise. When you Pin<Box<dyn Future>>, you're telling the type system that the future will never outlive the current execution frame.
Okay, so I understand that part. But what exactly happens when we call Poll on this boxed future? Does it actually interact with our context and waker fields?

When you poll the Pin<Box<dyn Future>>, the implementation of Poll is invoked which reads from your executor's internal state. Your executor maintains a context that holds the current waker, and when the future polls successfully it returns Ready with the value plus updates the waker to reflect any new I/O readiness.
I see how the waker gets updated, but why do we need a separate context field if the future itself manages its own state?

The future doesn't manage everything because it needs to delegate back to the executor when it's ready. The context holds the waker so that when you wake up the executor, it knows which specific future is waiting and can resume polling immediately without scanning all futures again.
That makes sense for the architecture, but I'm still confused about how we actually build this simple executor from scratch. What's the first step?

The first step is defining a struct that holds your list of futures and your current waker. You need to implement a method that takes a Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and returns a Result with either Ready or Pending, then you'll use that in a loop to drive the execution.
I'm trying to write out the Poll implementation but I keep getting errors related to the dyn trait. Is there something wrong with my approach?

The error is likely because you're forgetting that Pin<Box<dyn Future>> requires a specific lifetime and the future must implement Unpin if it's not wrapped in Box. When you define your executor, make sure the future type is strictly boxed and unconditionally pinned before passing it to Poll.
Wait, I thought dyn Future already handled that? Why am I still getting errors?

You're right about the trait bound but not about the Pin requirement. Dyn Future doesn't automatically make a type Unpin unless you explicitly add Unpin to its bounds or wrap it in Box. That's why Pin<Box<dyn Future>> is mandatory here even though it seems redundant at first glance.
Okay, I've added the Unpin bound but now my executor isn't waking up correctly when a future completes. What's the issue?

The issue is probably that you're not updating the waker after each Poll call. When a future returns Ready, you must construct a new waker instance that reflects its readiness and then assign it to your executor's context before continuing the next iteration.
I've been updating the waker but it feels like I'm doing it wrong. The documentation says something about wake scheduling?

You're doing it correctly, but you might be missing that the waker needs to be a closure or function that can actually trigger the resume of your executor loop. Wake scheduling means ensuring that when the future completes, the signal reaches back to your main polling thread.
I'm getting frustrated with this async manual polling thing. It feels like a lot of boilerplate for what should be simple.

That frustration is common because manual polling does require more code than standard async/await, but understanding Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and how it interacts with context and waker gives you full control. Once you grasp the executor loop, wake scheduling becomes straightforward.
Can we do a quick example? I want to see exactly how Pin<Box<dyn Future>> fits into the executor struct.

Sure, here's the core structure: your executor holds a Vec<Pin<Box<dyn Future>>> and a Waker instance. Your poll method iterates through the vector, calls Poll on each boxed future, updates the waker when Ready is returned, then repeats until all futures complete or an error occurs.

I've got a working solution using Pin<Box<dyn Future>> to wrap my executor loop, and I'm manually calling poll on the context. It compiles fine but feels clunky compared to tokio's built-in runtime. Is there a better way to handle this without reinventing the wheel?

You're right that manual polling with Pin<Box<dyn Future>> is verbose and error-prone, especially when managing wakers. The standard approach in Rust ecosystems is to use a runtime like tokio or smol which handles the executor logic internally. These runtimes manage the Poll state and waker registration automatically so you don't have to implement the wake scheduling yourself.

I understand, but I'm curious why people build custom executors with Pin<Box<dyn Future>> instead of just using a crate. What specific problems does solving manually actually address that a library won't?

The primary advantage is control over the scheduling mechanism itself. With a custom executor, you can implement non-standard wake-up triggers or modify how Poll interacts with your application's context. This is useful when you need fine-grained control over thread affinity or want to experiment with different polling strategies beyond what standard runtimes offer.
That makes sense for experimentation, but in production? If I'm building a simple executor with Pin<Box<dyn Future>>, what are the risks compared to just calling spawn_blocking or using tokio::spawn?

The main risk is correctness. When you manually manage context and waker, it's easy to miss edge cases where a future needs to be polled again after being notified. You must ensure every Future implements the Send + Sync traits correctly and that your wake logic respects ownership rules enforced by Pin<Box<dyn Future>>.
So I'd need to handle the waker registration myself? Like calling Arc::new(Waker) in my executor loop?

Exactly. You create an Arc<()>, clone it into each Future that needs a wake signal, and store the Arc in your context. When you want to notify a future, you drop the Arc or trigger its waker, which causes the Future's poll method to return Ready.
Can you show me how that fits into Pin<Box<dyn Future>> specifically? I'm trying to understand where the Box comes in and why it's necessary here instead of just using Arc<dyn Future>?

Pin is required because Futures must be pinned to prevent their internal state from being moved, which could cause unsafe behavior during polling. Pin<Box<dyn Future>> ensures the future is always pin-compatible regardless of its concrete type, while Arc<dyn Future> would fail to compile if the dynamic trait object wasn't properly pinned.
Got it, so I need to wrap my future in Pin<Box<>> before passing it to my executor loop. But then how do I actually run this without blocking the thread?

You create a loop that repeatedly polls your pinned future until it returns Ready, then handle any side effects. For non-blocking execution across threads, you'd typically spawn this executor on a separate thread or use async/await macros to delegate the polling logic into an async function.
I see. But wait, if I'm using Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and manually polling, why isn't this just equivalent to running a simple async block in tokio? What's the extra complexity really worth?

The complexity is worth it only when you need custom scheduling behavior or want to avoid runtime dependencies. However, for most applications using standard Rust patterns, sticking with async/await and crates like tokio or smol provides the same functionality without the boilerplate of manually managing Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and wakers.

I wrote a simple executor using impl Future and Pin but I'm manually polling the state with if let Some(_) = self.poll(). Is there a better way to handle this instead of writing out these manual poll checks every time?

You're already on the right track by using Pin and impl Future, but you can simplify things significantly with async/await syntax. Instead of manually calling self.poll() in a loop, you'd just use .await on your future objects. This lets the compiler handle the state machine transitions automatically without you having to write explicit polling logic.
But I need manual control over when things wake up and get scheduled. Can't I just use std::future::Future directly with a custom executor instead of async/await?

You can absolutely do that, but it's much more verbose. When you stick to the standard Future trait and impl Future pattern, you lose some syntactic sugar while gaining full control over waker registration and context management.

I see. So if I want fine-grained control over wake scheduling without async/await abstraction, I should implement the Future trait manually with Poll::Ready or Poll::Pending? And then wrap it in a Box<dyn Future>?

Exactly. By implementing impl Future for your type and returning Poll::Ready or Poll::Pending, you get full control over the execution flow. Then wrapping it with Pin<Box<dyn Future>> allows you to store multiple futures on the stack and manage them efficiently.
Wait, so I'm not using async/await at all in my executor? That seems counterintuitive since everything is async these days. Why would I avoid the convenience?

Good question. The manual approach gives you explicit control over waker registration and state transitions, which matters when building custom executors or high-performance systems. Async/await is just syntactic sugar on top of this same underlying Future trait implementation.
I understand now but I'm still confused about how context and waker fit in. Can you explain the relationship between Pin, Box<dyn Future>, and how things get scheduled?

When you pin a future with Pin<Box<dyn Future>>, you guarantee it won't move during polling. The executor then tracks which futures are pending and registers their wake callbacks to notify them when the event loop becomes available again.
Ah, so I'm building a mini-runtime where I manually manage the waker list? That's actually more powerful than just using async/await in a macro?
Precisely. You're creating a lightweight runtime that gives you direct access to the scheduling mechanism rather than relying on the standard library's async runtime.
I'm getting this error: cannot infer type for opaque type Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output=()>>> in context of a simple executor. I think I forgot to pin the future correctly.

That's a classic sign that your Future isn't pinned before being stored or returned. You need to wrap your Future in Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output=()>>> so it can be safely moved and polled later. Think of the Pin trait like a lock on a door; without it, the compiler thinks the data might move around while you're trying to read it.
Okay, I wrapped it but now my executor is just sleeping forever. The waker isn't being called when items should complete.

You're likely not properly implementing the wake scheduling logic in your loop. A proper executor needs to track which futures are pending and only poll them when their associated waker is notified. If you're just polling everything blindly, you'll miss events.
I added a waker to the future but I'm still not getting the right behavior in my test cases.

The issue is probably that you're not actually storing the waker somewhere accessible. You need to keep track of which futures are pending and how many are complete so you can properly schedule wakeups when needed.
I'm tracking completions but my executor still seems to miss some events in the test cases.
Think of it like a restaurant kitchen; you need to track which dishes are ready and notify the customers. If your waker isn't being notified when a future completes, that's where the bug is.

I'm trying to build a simple task runner in Rust that wraps arbitrary async code. I've been stuck on how to manually poll the future without relying on tokio or any standard library runtime. Here is what I have so far: let mut f = Pin::new(my_future()); while true { match Poll::<Result<(), Error>>::poll(&mut f) { Poll::Ready(v) => return v, Poll::Pending => continue } }

That logic looks mostly correct for the core loop. The main issue is that you're not using a `Pin` on your future before passing it to `poll`. You need to wrap the future in a `Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = Result<(), Error>> + Send>>` so the compiler enforces pinning and allows you to manually drive the state machine. Your current code tries to poll an unboxed or non-pinned type, which will fail at compile time.

Ah okay, I see. So I should use `Pin::new()` on my future, then box it? But wait, if I just do `let f = Pin::new(my_future());`, isn't that already pinned? Why do I need the extra `Box<dyn Future>` part?

Good catch. `Pin::new()` does pin a concrete type, but your future might have generic parameters or specific trait bounds that make it non-`Future<Output = ...>`. The `Box<dyn Future>` approach is the standard way to handle a heterogeneous set of futures because you can't know what concrete type they are at runtime. You need a dynamic trait object so you can store them in a vector and poll each one independently.
Right, but how do I actually implement the wake scheduling? My code just loops forever until `Poll::Ready`. How does it ever stop or get interrupted?

You need a separate thread that calls `waker::wake()` whenever an external event occurs. The waker is part of the executor infrastructure. You create a `Waker` object, attach it to your future via `Future::poll`, and then spawn a thread that monitors some condition and triggers the wake when needed. This allows the manual poll loop to resume after being paused.
So I need two threads, one for polling and one for waking? And what exactly do I pass to `waker::wake()`?

Yes, typically a producer-consumer pattern. The poll thread calls `Future::poll` on your boxed future, which returns `Poll::Pending`. When that happens, you store the waker reference in a variable. Then the wake thread grabs that waker and calls its `wake()` method to signal the poll thread to resume. You don't pass anything specific other than invoking the waker's interface.
Got it. So my structure would be something like: let f = Pin::new(Box::new(my_future())); and then in the loop I check `match Poll::<...>::poll(&mut f)`. Is that right?

Almost. You don't pin a Box again; you just box your future once after creating it from `Pin::new()`. Your loop should look like: let mut f = Pin::new(Box::new(my_future())); while true { match Poll::<Result<(), Error>>::poll(&mut f) { ... } }. The key is ensuring the type inside matches the generic expected output of your poll function.
One more thing, what if my future returns different error types? Should I map them all to a common Error?

Yes, you should define a unified `Error` type and use `map_err` in your `Future::poll` implementation. This keeps the executor clean by ensuring every poll attempt returns a consistent result format. You can also consider using `anyhow` or `thiserror` crates to simplify error handling across your tooling.

Thanks for clarifying. So my final executor looks like this: struct Executor { f: Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = Result<(), Error>> + Send>> }; impl Executor { pub fn run(&mut self) -> Result<(), Error> { loop { match Poll::<Result<(), Error>>::poll(&mut self.f) { ... } } } }

That's very close. You might want to add a break condition instead of an infinite loop if you only have one future running, or manage multiple futures with a vector and track which ones are pending. Also, ensure the `Executor` struct holds the waker so you can trigger it externally when needed.
Okay, I'll refactor that now. One last question: is there any performance penalty compared to using tokio's runtime?

There might be some overhead since you're managing the waker and polling state manually without the scheduler optimizations in tokio. However, for simple single-threaded or low-latency tasks where you want full control over scheduling, this manual approach can be just as efficient or even more predictable. It avoids the context switching overhead of a full event loop.
Makes sense. I think I have enough to start coding now. Thanks for walking me through the waker and Pin details!
You're welcome! Don't hesitate to reach out if you hit any compile errors or logic bugs while implementing the wake signal. Happy coding!

I'm trying to implement a simple executor using Pin<Box<dyn Future>> but I can't get the waker to trigger correctly. Here's my current setup: let task = Box::pin(my_future()); let mut pin = Pin::new(&mut *task); while !task.is_done() { poll_next(&mut pin); } where poll_next tries to call the Poll method on the future but the state isn't updating properly.

The issue likely stems from how you're handling the mutable reference and the waker registration. When you Pin a Box<dyn Future>, the internal mutability changes, so you need to ensure your Poll method correctly updates the context before returning Ready or Pending. Specifically, you should be calling poll_next on the pinned future object itself rather than trying to mutate it externally without proper synchronization.

Ah, I see now that my code was mutating the wrong reference. But even after fixing that, when I return Pending, the waker isn't being notified. Let me show you what's happening: inside poll_next, I check if there's work to do and call a custom wake function, but it seems like the executor never actually calls the waker because my Poll implementation doesn't properly set up the context.

That's exactly right. The problem is that when you return Pending from Poll, you must ensure the waker gets registered in the executor's scheduling mechanism before returning. Your current code probably returns Pending without first calling wake_on_pending on the executor structure itself.

I think I misunderstood how the waker interacts with Pin<Box<dyn Future>>. I thought the waker was just a callback, but it seems like it needs to be stored somewhere accessible during polling. Can you clarify the relationship between the waker and the executor state?

The waker is actually part of the executor's internal state that tracks which futures are ready or pending. When a future returns Pending, it signals the executor to call wake_on_pending so the waker can be registered in the scheduler. The Pin<Box<dyn Future>> wrapper ensures that when you mutate the future, you're working with a stable reference that doesn't invalidate the waker.

Okay, but I'm still confused about how to structure the executor loop. Should I be manually calling wake_on_pending every time poll returns Pending? And what happens if multiple futures are pending at once?

Yes, you must explicitly call wake_on_pending whenever Poll returns Pending for each future being polled. If multiple futures are pending simultaneously, your executor should maintain a queue or set of pending futures and iterate through them to ensure all get properly scheduled.
That makes sense now. But I'm wondering if there's a simpler pattern for this than manually managing the waker registration every time. Are there any standard crates that handle this automatically?

For manual executor implementations like yours, you'll need to manage the waker registration yourself using Pin<Box<dyn Future>> and the Poll trait directly. However, if you're looking for production-ready solutions, crates like tokio or async-std handle this internally with their built-in event loops.

Got it. So my manual approach requires careful attention to how Pin works with Box<dyn Future> and the waker lifecycle, but I can see how that's different from using a mature runtime. Let me try refactoring this with proper wake_on_pending calls.

Exactly. The key insight is understanding that Poll returns Pending only when the executor needs to schedule the waker, not just as an error condition. Your implementation should follow this pattern: poll -> check result -> if Pending then wake_on_pending -> loop again.

I've updated my code with proper wake_on_pending calls and it's working! The executor now correctly schedules futures when they return Pending. But I noticed something odd - sometimes the waker gets registered twice even though only one future should be pending at a time.

That's likely because you're calling wake_on_pending multiple times in your loop without checking if the waker is already registered. You should maintain a flag or check whether the executor has already scheduled this particular future before attempting to register it again.
